Pilot was not able to maintain altitude and continued descending. We were looking for a safe place for the helicopter to land. Also; wanted to keep aircraft out of the clouds due to icing. Aircraft X requested priority handling. I tried to assist the best way I could due to the conditions. Pilot reported being in moderate to heavy icing conditions and was unable to maintain altitude.

In Cruise Flight at FL380 approaching ZZZZZ north boundary of ZZZZ airspace we were denied right deviations for active military airspace. Left deviations at that point were no longer an option and appeared worse. We were on the western edge of a line of weather moving East we were deviating just slightly left of our course to go through what appeared to be the thinnest area of weather on our radar. I had made a PA and had all passengers and Flight Attendants seated. We experienced a brief 10 to 15 seconds of Moderate Turbulence which cause our Altitude to drop 200 ft. to about 37;800 ft. and the a gain of about 300 ft. to about 38;100 ft. and then settled back to level at FL380. We also experienced some airspeed loss and then gain briefly as well as some left bank. It felt much like wake turbulence briefly. Just then MIA center told us to maintain FL380 and contact ZZZZ; before leaving the frequency I stated we had experienced some moderate turbulence which had caused some altitude loss; a couple hundred feet; and that we were now level at FL380 and we were told 'roger' contact Center which we did. The remainder of the flight was uneventful and smooth to destination. [The cause of the event was that] a deviation 100's of miles earlier to the left would have been a better choice and I believe a couple of aircraft behind us after hearing my report to MIA Center deviated into the Military Airspace. I do not know if the aircraft behind me declared an Emergency to do so or not; as we were changing frequency to next center during the radio discussions. That may have been a better choice but I did not expect the turbulence to be that bad; but was very glad I sat the Flight attendants down prior; as well as making a very clear PA to the passengers that we were going to experience some Turbulence since we had to fly in the area of some weather and insisted that they be seated with their seatbelts securely fastened. An earlier plan for weather deviations 100's of mile away if necessary would have helped knowing any right deviations would be denied.
Report_2_Narrative: Level at 380 approaching XXXXX; there was some weather close to the intersection. Requested right deviation and Center informed that Warning Areas were active so unable right deviation. We encountered some moderate turbulence in the clouds for a few seconds. The airplane descended 200 ft. before returning to level. Miami informed to maintain 380 and to contact next center over ZZZZ which we did. Flight continued uneventfully.The cause of deviations was not being able to deviate due to Center's instructions.I suggest deviating ahead of time if known that no deviations allowed.

Report_1_Narrative: Prior to landing; ATIS received reported winds at 060@06KTS; 3/4SM -SN OVC004 with braking action good. Upon talking to Tower; the controller also stated that an aircraft landing approximately an hour or so earlier reported braking action good with no further reports. Upon starting the approach; we had ground contact at 3;000 ft. On about a 6-mile final to the runway the snow and rain mix got very heavy. At approximately 700 ft. we broke out with the runway in sight. Upon touching down and applying the brakes I felt and saw the aircraft begin to skid to the left as if the main wheels were sliding. Upon releasing brake pressure gently and using rudder inputs we regained directional control. Upon applying the brakes again and now the thrust reversers spooled up; the wheels slid again and abruptly caused the tail of the aircraft to yaw to the left again in a skidding motion. I then regained directional control of the aircraft with rudder inputs to regain centerline. Once I regained directional control and gently getting the brakes to work without locking up; reverse thrust was activated again with some effect and slowing the aircraft down. Upon reaching 500 ft. of runway remaining I had positive brake control. However; as slippery as the runway was; I could not make the right turn at the end of the runway onto the taxiway which would have been a 45 to 90-degree turn. making that turn with still some speed I believe would have caused the aircraft to go into an uncontrolled lateral skid causing damage to the aircraft and/or injury to passengers. The aircraft then gently departed the end of the runway about 20 ft. the aircraft did not sink or bog. We immediately contacted the Tower and the Flight Attendant. The Flight Attendant stated that everyone was ok with no injuries. Afterwards we contacted Dispatch; Operations; and the Chief Pilot. Every five minutes or so I updated the passengers with what happened and that we were working on getting them off the aircraft. The Flight Attendant did an excellent job assisting the passengers. Operations transported the passengers to the terminal. During the deplaning of passengers; Contract Maintenance inspected the aircraft and found no damage to the aircraft. Braking action was not reported correctly. Under no circumstances was that runway good as reported. The aircraft that landed an hour or so ahead of us may have had good braking action but how much snow and rain fell within that hour? Contract maintenance told me that the reason he was on the field was due to [another] flight; which had a flap failure after landing due to the amount of snow; ice and slush trapped in the flaps after landing.I suggest runway inspections every hour.
Report_2_Narrative: As we approach to terminal segment of the flight; I submit a WX request through the ACARS and confirm it with the current broadcasting ATIS. The ATIS was reporting wind at 060 @ 6 kts; 3/4sm -SN OVC 400 with a braking action reported as 'good'. Also took notice at the Notam indicated the Ficon as 5/5/5. While checking in with Tower the Controller was very vague on the runway condition stating a past aircraft was able to land. At about 3;000 ft. I told the Captain that I could see some residential lights in the distance just for situational awareness. At around 700-800 ft. I was able to spot the runway approach lights; calling 'continue'; and about 3-5 second after I was able to see the runway and runway edge lights calling 'runway in sight.' The flare to the round out part of the landing were standard in nature: On speed; on centerline and on glidepath.As we touched down the Captain started applying brake and the aircraft skidded to left of centerline as if we were skidding on locked wheels. Soon after; I saw the Captain making adjustments and bring the aircraft back to the center line. Additionally; as I looked down the runway I saw with my peripheral [vision] when [the Captain] was deploying the thrust reversers and seconds after I saw them spool up. As I felt that neither the reverse thrust nor the antiskid were helping us to come to a full stop; I looked down the runway then over to the yoke just in case the Captain needed me to take over the yoke to use the tiller. This wasn't the case. On the rollout the brakes kept locking up several times and the plane kept veering to the left. [The Captain] kept applying the correction to keep on centerline. [He] kept repeating that process until he was able to regain efficient braking from the brake while keeping directional control of the aircraft.Although there was a turn at the end of the runway the Captain made the decision on keeping the aircraft [aligned] with the runway to avoid an uncontrolled spin or ground loop due to the momentum we still had and the runway condition. The aircraft then came to a full stop at the end of the runway with both main and nose in the grass and tail and rudder were still over the runway (about 20 ft.). We both coordinated with the Tower Controller; Operations; Dispatch and Flight Attendant to ensure that all the passenger were safe. The braking action was not reported correctly on the ATIS. As we locked wheels a couple of times it became apparent to us that the runway condition was 'poor.' Even thought it was reported 'good' and Ficon 5/5/5 we got little to no braking action from the runway. Also; the constant locking and sliding of the wheels made it a lot harder to come to a full stop. Lastly; it didn't seem as it was light snow although that was reported. As far as correction the airport should be more specific on the runway condition. Plow more often when the intensity of the snow increases. This event could have been avoided if we had known the actual condition of the runway.

Report_1_Narrative: Landed XX back taxied on XX; right turn onto runway XY/XZ. Airport maintenance advised via CTAF to back taxi XY/XZ with a left turn due to snow drifts that had developed very quickly. While back taxiing on runway XY/XZ due to lack of tire traction and rudder effectiveness because of runway ice and wind were unable to make the turn onto [the] taxiway. The aircraft would not turn and the wind was causing it to slide to the left due to the crosswind component. In response; the safest course of action was to shut down the engine to eliminate the pulling thrust vector to stop the aircraft and let it weathervane into the wind. This successfully brought the aircraft to a controlled stop. We contacted airport maintenance on Unicom stating that we require a tug to the hanger. At this point airport maintenance informed us that they were placing a NOTAM that runways were closed. Passengers were picked up and taken to the terminal in a crew car. We secured the aircraft for tow while waiting for a suitable tug to tow the aircraft. Airport maintenance had informed us that they intended on closing the runways before we arrived but were distracted with task of snow plowing. The aircraft was tugged back to the terminal and stowed safely in the hangar.The combination of wind and ice on the airport surface compromised the ability to safely taxi the aircraft on the surface under its own power; requiring a tug.The only way to stop the aircraft due to nil braking action was to shut off the engine because even at idle the aircraft produced enough thrust to slide forward with brakes applied. This allowed the aircraft to weather vane and come to a complete stop over the runway.Airport maintenance [should] be more prompt with runway and taxiway closures.

I was working Local 2 and Ground Control combined during slow traffic and the airport was in a North flow deice configuration.  The aircraft were landing XXL with med-good braking action reports from pilots.  Aircraft X was given a clearance to land with a med-good braking action report.  Aircraft X rolled quickly pass to high speeds so I figured he was trying to roll to the end quickly.  I instructed Aircraft X to turn left at the end.  At that point he was approaching the end at a good rate of speed.  I sent the next aircraft around as I watched Aircraft X go slightly passed the end of the runway; make a hard left turn back onto taxiway M from M10 taxiway.  Aircraft X verified that he was off of runway XXL so L let the next aircraft continue and land without any issues.  Aircraft X asked to have airport vehicle to check his aircraft in case he had hit any taxiway lights.  The airport closed runway XXL.  I later observed Aircraft X taxi back to the gate on his own power.Other aircraft seemed to be able to make the high speed taxiways without any issue.  Maybe if the pilot came in with a slower approach speed it could have helped him not to roll long and slide.
Report_2_Narrative: Completed all briefings prior to approach. We were expecting a VFR approach but briefed like full IFR approach and were prepared for the weather to change. We were preparing for a normal approach and landing to Runway XXL. ATIS Bravo showed three miles and overcast 4100 feet. ATIS BRAVO also advised RCC CODE 5-5-5 for Runways XXR; XXL; XYL. Planned a flap 15 landing. We used conservative number of CODE 4 RCC giving us Autobrakes 2; 1530 stopping margin. In essence 3000 feet; touchdown zone being the most restrictive of the 12;000 foot runway. On final approach;  Tower stated Braking Action Good to RJ in front of us.We landing uneventfully. I believe we landed within 3;000 TDZE; however; I do remember floating a little with 10 knots ice additive. We did not have any markings visible outside center line do to white snow. Decelerated to 60 knots; F/O called 60 knots. I stowed the thrust reversers and pressed brakes to disconnect autobrakes. Nothing seemed unusual at that point. Deceleration rate seemed normal up to that point. Tower stated: 'Roll to end; aircraft behind you two-mile final.'I depressed brakes to further decelerate and realized there was zero braking available. I stated 'No brakes' to F/O a couple of times. F/O immediately told Tower and aircraft behind us went around. I extended full reverse thrust again to try and get aircraft stopped. We drifted about 45 degrees toward intended exit. We never left hard surface of runway/taxiway. I briefed F/O to contact Ground immediately and notify 'NILL BRAKING LAST THIRD OF RUNWAY XXL.'I also had F/O ask for inspection of runway/taxiway lights to make sure aircraft had no damage before we taxied any further. He also notified Company Ops what had just occurred. I briefed what had happened to Passengers on PA. ZZZ Ground Ops notified there were no runway/taxiway lights damaged. We taxied aircraft to gate without any further issues. Maintenance was waiting for us at gate and we described what had occurred. They inspected aircraft and found an actual blue taxiway light laying on top of wheel carriage on right main. There was no aircraft damage noted by the Maintenance inspection. I put a note in logbook and we deadheaded to ZZZ1.I take briefings very seriously. This was not the type of flight that either Pilot was out of loop or just not having a bad day. We were configured at the marker and on speed the whole time on approach. I may have floated a little do to 10 knot increase of VREF. The ATIS reports from ZZZ were RCC 5-5-5. The two previous aircraft reports BRAG. The only difference was; the two previous aircraft exited 2nd high speed. We rolled past the second high speed for passenger comfort. We never expected NIL braking under light snow covered runway in front of us. The airport needs to actually test the runway; if they're going to advertise 5-GOOD. It was one of the worst feelings a Pilot can have realizing he/she has lost effective control of an aircraft. Had I not redeployed the thrust reversers; I believe we would have encountered aircraft damage and maybe even injuries even though aircraft would have been exiting hard surface at a slow speed. I am actually shocked the runway had not been prepared for arrivals with so much equipment and men available at that airport. Very disappointed.

Report_1_Narrative: Flying into ZZZ. Because of the weather we setup for a CAT 2 to Runway XXL. I briefed the CAT 2 off the pages. We were cleared for the approach. I armed the approach. CAT 3; dual. I was looking outside to pick up the runway. We got the Land green. I picked up the lead in lights then the runway. I said landing. At approximately 50 feet the aircraft said retard. I retarded the thrust levers. At approximately 30 feet and descending quickly I realize the aircraft was not going to flare. I pulled back on the stick and we landed firmly. We taxied to the gate. I called the maintenance control person and explained what went on. He look at the data. He said there was a 'momentary glitch' in the FMC. I don't know if we received a flare display and neither does the FO. I think when I said landing the FO also started looking outside. We debriefed the approach and landing sequence. I think also so many of our FOs are very distracted because of the virus situation; the furloughs; etc. If we were in the military it would be a good time for a Safety Stand-down. I understand we can't do that; but it is very tough out here for the line pilots; FA; Gate Agents etc. [I suggest] ensuring everyone does their job all the way to engine shutdown.
Report_2_Narrative: On descent into ZZZ we were prepared for an ILS approach to [Runway] XXR when the weather was reporting below minimums for the approach. 3;000 RVR was reported and 4;000 RVR was needed. We requested an approach to XXL which has a CAT 2 ILS approach and were vectored around for the approach. The approach was loaded and CAT 2 autoland procedures briefed. The approach was normal breaking out at about 200 feet with blowing snow but fairly good visibility. As we approached the 40 foot flare point I was distracted and not looking at the PFD. The Captain noticed the aircraft was not flaring and turned off the autopilot and landed the airplane firmly.We briefed the autoland approach and I quickly reviewed the required callouts. I was distracted when we entered visual conditions and my sight was drawn outside the aircraft. I missed the flare annunciation and not sure if it was presented. Captain noticed the lack of flare and took the appropriate action landing the aircraft. If I had called out no flare at 40 feet a go-around probably would have been more appropriate. When conducting autoland approaches the First Officer needs to remain focused on the flight mode annunciator during the whole approach all the way through rollout. Emphasis needs to be placed on this importance during the briefing. We rarely conduct autoland approaches and thus can be overlooked along with the natural tendency to want to look up and out the window when going visual on an approach.

Report_1_Narrative: IOE (initial operating) student was attempting a flaps 22 landing. During the approach; gusty winds from outer bands of an approaching hurricane contributed to an unstable approach and generating two glide slope EGPWS annunciations. We initiated a missed approach and were put in sequence to reattempt the approach. The vectors from the second approach brought us in above the glideslope. We engaged the vs (vertical speed) mode initially at 1;200 feet per minute (per GOM) and then 1;000 feet per minute as we closed in on the glide slope and stabilized approach criteria. The student had NAV armed; not APP; which as PM (pilot monitoring) I should have caught. My attention was briefly diverted to reassessing our fuel; which was 200 pounds over final divert at the final approach fix. At 1;000; instruments cross checked; the GS (glideslope) was exact (though not captured). At this point; we had been in visual conditions for several minutes. The runway did not have visual guidance (PAPI nor VASI); I looked out the window to verify the runway was clear; of which I annunciated. At approximately 700 feet AGL; we fell below the glide slope; generating additional glide slope EGPWS annunciations. Due to the winds; fuel nature; incoming weather; and diversion airport expecting similar conditions; I used my command authority to take control of the airplane; reestablish ourselves on the glide path; and land without further incident.

On final approach at an altitude of approximately 50 feet AGL we experienced a sudden shift in horizontal wind speed that landed the balloon in the front yard of a residence at [Address]. The basket landed beside the front of the residence creating minor damage to a window ledge and downspout on a gutter. There were no injuries to passengers or pilot and none to people on the ground as it was early in the morning with no people outside. The police and fire department responded and a police report was submitted. The residence was inspected by the fire department and found to have minimal cosmetic damage. No structural damage was found. There was no damage to the balloon or it's structural components. Arrangements have been made to correct the cosmetic damage to the residence with the owners representative.

We were cruising along when we experienced moderate to severe turbulence. The aircraft was temporary out of control and we lost about 800 feet in altitude over a period of about 10 seconds. The controller noticed that we had lost altitude and gave us a descent to FL450. The remainder of the flight was uneventful. Possible clear air turbulence. If we could get more pilot reports from the altitudes that we normally fly at that would be helpful. We did let the controller know that it was an extremely rough ride at Flight Level 470.

While flying at FL230; we began experiencing some moderate turbulence. The plane began an uncontrolled descent for 500 feet; and we reported it to ATC who then cleared us to FL220. The aircraft was controllable at FL220; but were still encountering moderate turbulence. We requested lower and were given FL210; and no more moderate turbulence was encountered. Turbulence in the flight levels was the primary causal factor. There was also active MOAs and crossing traffic that required us to stay higher. Upon getting the flight release I used my personal software of Foreflight to realize we were filed through a SIGMET for severe turbulence. We were then filed lower after talking with the Dispatcher. When talking to Center; they said there weren't any severe SIGMETS for our route of flight. I am not sure where the disconnect was; but I would suggest providing Dispatch the tools for greater awareness of flight through severe turbulence; as this was the second time in two days where we were filed through severe turbulence SIGMETS. Also; there were MOAs that we were being vectored over by one sector of Center; and then another sector said they were cold. This could have possibly been coordinated better to allow for lower altitude to stay out of turbulence.

We were filed at 21;000 feet. Center required that we climb to 23;000 feet to overfly the Military Operation Area enroute. As we entered the turbulence the aircraft indicated an uncommanded descent and an increase of airspeed towards the overspeed zipper. We ended up 500 feet below our assigned altitude. We contacted Center to report our encounter and asked for a lower altitude to depart the turbulent air. They obliged and also took our PIREP regarding our turbulence encounter. Given we had lost 500 feet and could do nothing to stop this it is my opinion that we were in severe turbulence. Once on the ground; we wrote up the aircraft for inspection and then notified the [Chief Pilot].

Aircraft X [flight] got handed off from ZLC they passed along that they were low fuel. Aircraft X was a flight of 6. With the weather being poor and the visibility being 4 miles an instrument approach is what they were requesting. That being said; their TACAN and ILS were both out of service. The only way they were getting in was a visual to [Runway] XXR. So I vectored the flight behind the [civilian] aircraft coming from the south. Giving them block altitudes above the MVA so they had proper spacing amongst themselves. As I was stepping Aircraft X down they kept wanting lower and lower in order to get the visual approach. I stepped them down to 5000 and they descended to 4800; I gave them an LOA (low altitude alert). Then after fixing that issue I gave them 4500 once they were in the 4500 MVA. Turned them to final and I noticed that they dropped below 4500. So I gave them another LOA and told them to maintain 4500. I asked them numerous times if they had the airport in sight. They said no so I climbed them to 8000 and told them to fly heading 100. Aircraft X flight 2-5 reported the field in sight and at that point I cleared them for the visual. Aircraft X 6 didn't get the airport so I turned him away from the mountains and climbed them. After that I gave him a new squawk code and split Aircraft X 1 and Aircraft X 6 up. After clearing Aircraft X 6 to ZZZ and verifying his altitude I sequenced the two flights to get the visual behind another arrival. When we have airplanes that are low fuel and have no approaches we need to get those approaches fixed. Tech ops or who ever was fixing them need to understand the situation on hand.

On a relatively calm and clear day at ZZZ1; I departed southbound toward ZZZ. The takeoff was uneventful; I contacted Approach for flight following. Throughout the climb to 9;500 ft.; there was constant light to moderate turbulence; typical for this segment of flight throughout this area. Upon reaching cruise the severity increased to consistent moderate turbulence; again not abnormal for the route of flight; the aircraft was slowed. I reported the ride conditions to Approach; and they stated that a Cessna 208 had reported similar conditions and opted for lower. The airplane began to experience downdrafts; exceeding over 1000 fpm and airspeed fluctuations of +/- 20 knots. I elected to descend to 7;500 ft. based on the ride conditions reported by the aircraft in front of me. At 7;500 ft. I experienced consistent moderate to severe turbulence; with consistent up and downdrafts; accompanied by fluctuations in airspeed. Occasionally; I nearly hit my head on the ceiling of the airplane. I queried [ATC] and they stated that the aircraft had reported a better ride at 6000 ft.; at that time I descended to 6000 ft. At 6000 ft. the ride was much more manageable; and I was able to make it to ZZZ safely. I made the decision that the airplane did in fact fly through severe conditions and required an inspection. I wrote the airplane up and contacted Maintenance Control. The unstable nature of the valley I was flying over and the absence of weather reporting in the valley make it difficult to gauge the ride conditions. I should have slowed the airplane down; and descended to more manageable flight conditions.A solution would have been flying the airplane above the valley to avoid possible severe turbulence. I talked with my pilot supervisor and he advised that flying at 15;500 ft. over the valley is a viable option. This particular route is very abnormal; with respect to mitigating risk. This is a prime example of how quickly things can change when flying. The more we can do to try to mitigate these risks as a whole; the safer it will become.

There was a line of storms crossing ZZZ.  We were told to expect holding at ZZZZZ.  We ended up holding.  We did one turn and then we got sent back on the arrival.  We ultimately got vectors from ZZZZZ1 and were following one Company aircraft.  The plan was to fly northeast about 30 miles at 5;000 ft to go around the weather and then come back south to land.  We couldn't get in front of it or through it.  Company turned around to try to get around the weather from the other direction.  We did the same thing.  We got a 270 heading and a descent to 4;000 ft and an assigned speed of 170 knots.  On that vector; I noticed airspeed decreasing and added some power.  Then the airspeed dropped to approximately 140 knots and the autopilot and autothrottles were disconnected.  I announced to the PM (Pilot Monitoring) that we were in windshear and I added power and started a climbing turn to the left.  The aircraft started accelerating and climbing; and we retracted the flaps.  The highest speed I saw during the recovery was 220 knots.  During this period; airspeed; pitch attitude and altitude was very difficult to control and we encountered what we believed to be severe turbulence.  The PM told ATC that we had severe turbulence and windshear and that we were turning to the left and climbing.  We tried to level at 5;000 ft and flew southwest.  ATC told us to climb to 6;000 ft for traffic.  I added power and the aircraft responded poorly.  Eventually; we got a climb going; but we were unable to stop the climb at 6;000 ft.  The aircraft continued to about 6;450 ft and ATC asked us what altitude we were going to.  PM told them that we were trying to get to 6;000 ft but were having a difficult time maintaining altitude with the turbulence.  On our way back down to 6;000 ft we got a TCAS TA for traffic at our 1-2 o'clock at 700-800 ft above us.  I continued down to about 5;800 ft to make sure we avoided that traffic.  We continued southwest and diverted to ZZZ1. If we had asked to stay in holding until we saw that the line had passed even further northeast of the field; it would have prevented this.  We had about 13.0 of fuel when we entered holding; so we had the time. [We were] unable to maintain assigned altitude; heading; and airspeed due to windshear and severe turbulence.

Upon arriving at MSO airport on [Date] at approximately XA:00 the winds were not favorable to execute the ILS to RWY (Runway) 12. The weather conditions to perform the RNAV GPS D were alternating between ceiling and visibility going below minimum conditions. We were communicating with Dispatch along the way and were discussing diverting to our first alternate; which [was] Billings (BIL). The ceiling and visibility at MSO was above minimums at time of arrival. We started the RNAV approach with a circle to land on RWY 30. After crossing the final approach fix at 6;800 ft.; and descending to our MDA of 4;700 ft.; we started to have some ground contact. The FO (First Officer) said that he saw the PAPI lights to the right of us. The approach did not allow for us to circle to land northeast of RWY 12/30. The only way to commence the circle was to fly over the airport and circle around to the west. We briefly discussed circling to the west and quickly decided that it would be unsafe to circle around the airport at night in low visibility with LLWS (Low Level Wind Shear) conditions in mountainous terrain. During the decision making phase I had inadvertently disconnected the auto throttle. We continued to the missed approach point. As we went around I had realized that the MAP fix was not in the box. As we looked at the approach plate and quickly made a climbing left turn on a heading of about 340. We asked ATC for a heading while we headed in the proper direction. Once WENKE was back in the box we realized we were right on course.At approximately 8;000 ft. we were back in radar contact and was giving a vector off the final approach course. ATC asked us if we wanted to commence another approach or divert to a different airport. After discussing the conditions that we saw and weather forecast to be deteriorating; we unanimously decided that it would be unsafe to conduct a circle to land night approach based on the current weather and forecasted conditions.We then told ATC that we were going to divert to BIL. We sent ACARS message to Dispatch telling them we were diverting as well. After landing at BIL; Dispatch told us that we were to refuel and proceed back to MSO. Dispatch then said the weather was not good enough to go back and was delaying our departure. After waiting about an hour Dispatch said the winds were improving and we should be able to do the ILS RWY 12 back into MSO. We departed BIL for MSO with BIL as our alternate if we could not get in. We executed the ILS to RWY 12. While in Billings we contacted ATC and they told us at that time that they did not have radar until about 8;000 ft. The controller said that he was not working at that time but discussed with the previous controller that we were right where we were supposed to be and we said we just were at a high work load and wanted to verify with them that we were on course.High workload with low ceilings; LLWS; at night in mountainous terrain. Having to circle with visibility below what was projected and inadvertently disconnected auto throttles to land.I feel like this approach is not a safe approach to do at night. I spoke with several carriers that land at this airport and this approach is not authorized.

We were on arrival into ZZZ and on vectors with Approach Control. Prior to switching over to Approach Control ATC advised us that we could expect moderate turbulence; I believe from 9;000 to 12;000 feet; on descent. We were higher up on our descent when they notified us that we could expect the moderate turbulence in the lower altitude structure. We were switched over to approach control.  I remarked to approach that we were picking up moderate turbulence; again I believe we were at or above 6;000 feet to give them a heads up. We were given a left northerly turn to 010 degrees and a descent to 5;000 feet.  The Autopilot was engaged. It was very turbulent. We received an Alts Cap which is a normal indication on the FMA (Flight Mode Annunciator) that the autopilot is now armed to capture the selected altitude. We were getting bumped around pretty good by now; full continuous moderate turbulence. It was at that moment when the autopilot was capturing the altitude that the upset occurred. The aircraft was rocked to a 45 to 50 degree left bank and a sinking drop occurred similar to what you would receive in an airliner if you ever have hit an air pocket. This exceeded the limits of the autopilot and it kicked off. I took over manually to right the airplane and that is when we received what I would call a severe bump which led to the second upset that did the same thing; left bank to 45 to 50 degrees and a pitch downward.  I was already in the process of advancing power from the first upset but this time I went to full thrust; TOGA power if you will; to initiate the unusual attitude recovery.  I was aggressive in the recovery; put some G on the aircraft; airspeed bled down from 250 to about 180 or 190 at that moment which is still a safe flying airspeed in our type aircraft; well above stall.  I traded airspeed for altitude to expedite the recovery.  We recovered to altitude shortly after.This all happened so quickly.  Severe turbulence upset coupled with automation exceedance were contributing factors.  Lessons learned are significant. Turbulence is always a threat.Constance vigilance is always required.  Secondly we stress at our company to never fully rely on automation and that it will not always work in all situations.  We also stress that situations can happen to anyone regardless of experience. An Incident/Hazard Report has been filled out as part of our Safety Management System (SMS) program and submitted to the Director of Safety at our company. We train for upset situations every X months as part of our X month continuation qualification and training. I've flown many types of aircraft as well as fighter type aircraft and have never experienced a situation such as the one described above.

This is a pilot pushing issue.  Due to fog and visibility below minimums we could not get into [planned destination] last night. It was unforecasted and we did not have an alternate and/or fuel to try again so we diverted to [an alternate]. Shortly after arriving; as I was on the phone with Dispatch; someone walked in the cockpit and starts talking even though I was on the phone. I told him to hold on. I finish my call and he said; '[another air carrier] just landed in ZZZ1; so I was wondering when you were going to head back there. I was honestly taken aback. I told him; 'I don't know; the F/O and I have to look at the weather again'; and I would let him know. I conferred with the F/O (First Officer) who was comfortable trying again as was I; as well as the flight attendants (although one was going to run into a legality issue if the delay was any longer). However; I wanted to speak to the OPS guy. I called OPS and asked him to come back. At first he wanted to just talk on the radio; but I asked him to come to the cockpit. When he arrived; I let him have it. I told him that the weather was below legal landing minimums; and that flight landing after us was probably done illegally. The RVR when we went missed had dropped to 800 after we passed the FAF (Minimum RVR for landing on Runway X is 1800). I can assure you when we got to minimums we saw nothing. So the fact that Name1 comes up and tells me to essentially continue an illegal operation because another airline did it is pure and simple pilot pushing and unprofessional on so many levels.

While on arrival into Nashville; we were being vectored by Approach.  We were just assigned vectors outside of the arrival.  We were notified by the controlling agency that we could expect moderate turbulence between 9;000 and 12;000 feet in the descent; which we did encounter.  Upon reaching 5;000 feet; we were still encountering moderate turbulence.  As we accepted our vector to heading 010; we encountered a pocket of severe turbulence which sent the airplane into an upsetting unusual attitude.  My Captain immediately recognized the issue; as the autopilot kicked itself off; he initiated the proper recovery procedure.  As that happened; we encountered a second pocket of severe turbulence which further upset the aircraft attitude and resulted in additional altitude loss.  My Captain went full thrust and maximum nose up attitude to recover.  We experienced moderate turbulence all the way to the ground that day; but were able to land safely.

As I was flying to ZZZ; right about over MHT [VOR]; I encountered continues moderate turbulence at 6;000 feet; I requested to climb to 8;000 feet; as I was climbing ATC advised me that larger airplanes had reported moderate turbulence between 6;000 and 8;000 then; when I got out of the clouds at 8;000 feet; for a brief moment it got a little better; but; when I got in the clouds again; it got very aggressive turbulent again; the airplane made abrupt movements; life jackets coming loose and over the cabin; safety briefing card flying around and the passenger getting shacked back there; immediately I requested descent to 6;000 and then to 5;000; then at 5;000 I saw ZZZ airport; requested and got the visual approach and landed.

On approach to ZZZ; the ATIS was obtained with reported winds of 340 @ 15 G 28. The airport was configured to land east; utilizing Runways XL and XXC. The First Officer (FO) and I discussed how this was odd and not desirable as the tailwind component with gusts was at our 10 knot limitation. Additionally; the ATIS stated there were wind shear advisories in the area. On final approach inside the FAF; maintaining target airspeed within Vref +10 was extremely difficult due to the high wind gusts; shifting winds; and shear. As pilot flying; my airspeed was hovering at +10 with occasional jumps up to +20. The wind vector on the MFD was showing winds at 030 between 20-35 knots until 100 feet or so; where the winds sheared to 340 at an unknown speed. I touched down within the touchdown zone but off centerline by approximately 10 feet. Taxi to the hanger was uneventful and no damage was noted. During the debrief; the FO and I agreed a go-around with a request to land on a Westerly direction should have been made. The cause of this event were gusty winds; poor traffic flow decision by ATC; and undesirable speed control by the Pilot Flying due to windshear.  ATC decision to land to the east with reported winds of 340 @ 15 G 28. This decision by ATC caused an unsafe tailwind condition when coupled with very strong and gusty crosswinds. Additionally; myself or the FO should have requested a landing to the west. However; the First Officer and I determined that any request would be denied as ZZZ has a reputation for not accommodating these types of request in order to maintain efficient traffic flows. There is pressure to land with the prevailing flow of traffic at a major airport like ZZZ when the crew sees other aircraft landing in the existing conditions; even if the crew would prefer an alternate runway. I strongly believe ATC created an unsafe situation that may have inadvertently pressured pilots into landing with a tailwind in gusty conditions. When I made the decision to conduct the approach; a go around would have been the correct action when the wind sheared at 100 feet.

A line of scattered thunderstorms were moving west to east. We spoke with Clearance Delivery and Departure Control regarding the direction to go around these storms. Departure suggested we follow a flight which had just gone westbound to avoid the storms. We agreed. Departure Control said we should be given a turn in a few miles. We were then instructed to contact Center. The frequency was busy and they would not respond to our communications. We were at 23000 ft. and could not get Center to respond. We tried multiple times to check-in and ask for deviations; but no answer. We were using NEXRAD and on board radar. Our path was taking us through two storm cells separated by approximately 30 miles. The radar was showing light to moderate precipitation ahead of us. At no time did it show any red returns ahead of us. Center finally contacted us and told us to climb to 40000 ft. As we initiated the climb; we encountered moderate turbulence with approximately 10 seconds of severe turbulence. As we climbed through 26000 ft. we cleared the clouds and the turbulence abated. We had one passenger on board that was briefed on the expected turbulence and the seat belt sign was on. The passenger was seated with his seat belt on; but did hit his head on the ceiling of the cabin during the turbulence. He did not report any injuries. The cause of the problem was a saturated ATC system that couldn't handle the traffic load. Perhaps more controllers to handle the traffic load could alleviate the situation.

Departing during the waning stages of an intense storm. Winds were gusting close to 30 kts.. Windshear advisories were on the ATIS; however a flight landing 10 minutes before our departure reported only moderate turbulence with no mention of Windshear. Decided to let FO be the pilot flying. Climbing through 1;100 ft. MSL in a right turn to our cleared altitude of 4;000 ft.; the turbulence got very intense; with large; rapid; +or- 15 kts. fluctuations in airspeed. PF (Pilot Flying) called for flaps one; which I set; and thereafter he announced the autopilot was engaged. I had just changed to departure on the radio and then we were cleared to climb to 10;000 ft.; and to continue the right turn to our first navigation fix. PF called for flaps up; I selected the flaps up and then noticed the attitude of the airplane was falling below the horizon and we were starting to descend. I also noticed the PF trying to select 10;000 ft. on the altitude preselect. He was having difficulty setting the altitude selector as it was so turbulent ; so I stated: 'fly the airplane; we are descending'. The PF disconnected the autopilot and raised the nose after we reached about 3 degrees nose low attitude. During this time; our altitude dropped to about 1;300 ft. MSL (1;200 ft. AGL) and the GPWS 'Don't Sink' advisory sounded. We started to establish a climbing attitude so I directed my attention to setting the altitude preselect; setting the heading bug to our first fix; and the PF asked to also execute this fix in the FMS. After setting the FMS; I noticed our aircraft attitude was dropping below the horizon again and we were descending through 1;300 ft. MSL. Before I could command a correction; the GPWS 'Don't Sink' advisory sounded again; and the PF raised the nose. As we continued the climb; by this time the airspeed was climbing over 250 kts. and the aircraft attitude was raised to control the speed. The speed increased to approximately 287 kts; before starting to decrease. As the speed started to drop; the PF continued to raise the attitude of the airplane; and as we exceeded 25 degrees nose up I commanded the PF to 'lower the nose; our airspeed is decreasing; establish a pitch of 12 degrees'. By the time the airspeed stopped decreasing; our speed was about 5 kts. below the flaps up maneuvering speed. We accelerated and established a normal climb. From this point on; the rest of the climb was uneventful.

I had just landed the Piper PA 28 140 cruiser I have been approved to be flying solo by my CFI I just returned from training maneuvers like a tracking VOR's; slow flight; stalls etc. Then I came in for a landing and then after landing I decided to do a touch and go as I did I had a 90 degree crosswind that was coming at 5 knots which was well within my CFI's logbook approval for me. However after I had given it full throttle for the touch and go there was a gust of wind which lifted my left wing slightly. As I put left rudder in my foot inadvertently hit the break and caused my plane to pull to the left off of the runway at which time my nose gear collapsed which caused the incident with my airplane.

ZZZZZ3 Arrival into ZZZ; just over ZZZZZ1 Intersection; told by ATC to fly present heading; maintain 9;000 feet; and to expect Runway XXR due to weather moving east of the airport. Weather radar indicated moderate precipitation (no convection activity) for about 5 miles; no red and no turbulence indications. At that time we observed some static off the nose of the aircraft; then either a lightning strike or static discharge. We also experienced a shot duration of moderate turbulence; aircraft descended approximately 300 feet; then returned to assigned altitude. Flight landed without incident. No initial lighting damage noted by maintenance; but aircraft taken out of service for further inspection. WSI was also being utilized and no lightning was indicated in the vicinity of our aircraft flight path.

I was flying on an IFR flight plan at assigned altitude of 4000'. After seeing a cell on FIS-B radar (Flight Information System-Broadcast); I requested from ATC if there were any PIREPS for the weather. I then requested a deviation for weather depicted by FIS-B. I was granted the requested weather deviation and was flying outside of the weather depicted by FIS-B several miles in IMC conditions. Apparently; I flew into an embedded thunderstorm cell with both severe turbulence and violent downdrafts and updrafts which was not depicted by FIS-B weather with a 4 minute time stamp.I noticed the IAS had climbed dangerously high into the yellow arc while on autopilot (AP) maintaining an altitude of 4;000'. I reduced power to idle in an effort to maintain a safe indicated airspeed. While the AP was engaged and power reduced the IAS did not reduce sufficiently to the maneuvering speed but was rapidly climbing. I disconnected the AP and pulled the yoke back to reduce IAS. I then encountered severe turbulence and downdrafts forcing the aircraft to lose approximately 500 feet. In an attempt to stabilize the aircraft; I attempted to various throttle settings in an attempt to maintain altitude and heading with little success in controlling altitude or heading.The entire event lasted approximately 2 minutes with several calls from ATC offering assistance. During those calls I requested ATC standby until I was able to regain control of the aircraftThis was an isolated cell whose location was not accurately depicted by FIS-B weather radar returns. In regards to corrective actions on my part as pilot; I'd say to not trust FIS-B time stamps nor the weather depictions as being reasonably accurate. Further I'd extend my safety margin around weather depicted on FIS-B to a greater margin.The flight terminated without incident with my cancelling my IFR flight plan once out of IMC conditions and safely landing at the destination airport.

Our alternate was ZZZ2 exactly because ZZZ3; ZZZ4; ZZZ5; and ZZZ6 were not suitable weather-wise. The original flight plan had fuel to fly ZZZ-ZZZ1; go-around and fly to our alternate of ZZZ2 landing with a fairly standard REMF; as I recall; certainly not enough fuel to shoot a second approach in ZZZ1 and comfortably divert to ZZZ2.This was our third and final leg of the day; following a ZZZ-ZZZ7 turn. It was our second day of a three-day trip and we felt reasonably alert. If we had to divert to ZZZ2; I suspect we would have been close to timing out in our block time; from a passenger standpoint; ZZZ2 made a lot of sense with the ability to re-crew in a base. By the time we blocked in ZZZ1 we had completed 7:30 actual flight time. I know I was tired at the end of the day. Time compression in ZZZ between second and third leg.1. The flight plan for our final leg was not yet available for downloading and consideration before top of descent ZZZ7-ZZZ (leg #2). Flight planning on the ground felt very time compressed.2. We were switching aircraft in ZZZ.3. We were supposed to have been boarded crew meals for our second leg (ZZZ7-ZZZ) but for some odd reason; the FO's hot meal was boarded and I was offered a hodge-podge of a snack box; beef jerky; a cookie; three dried apricots and a packet of honey. I snacked on that offering but needed to find a real meal back in ZZZ between flights.4. Please appreciate the 64 minutes on the ground is from parking brake set on Flight number; (inbound flight); complete the parking checklist; collect our belongings;  traipse over to a different gate (thankfully; the gates were close); download and review: Flight Number's OFP; NOTAMs; and aircraft maintenance and history; review weather on WSI iPad app; pre-flight a new aircraft; load and check the FMC; brief flight attendants especially on the weather conditions; receive an ATC clearance; departure brief between pilots; accomplish both the pre-flight checklist and before push checklist; and then release the parking brake for push-back.Given the lower ceilings and reduced visibility with snow-rain mix and strong direct gusty crosswinds in the TAF I felt it prudent to add contingency fuel. I called our dispatcher at the gate in ZZZ and we agreed to add approximately 30 minutes of fuel; specifically; 3;000 pounds. I had hoped that our dispatcher was cognizant the conditions forecast in ZZZ1 warranted more consideration than a milk-run; day; VFR landing; but truthfully; I had a whisper of uneasy misgivings. Perhaps I felt this was the fuel plan SABRE popped out and there could have been more... more human QA to it... perhaps. Then again; maybe it was nothing... yet the voice in the back of my mind wasn't letting up. Something felt unsettled. Was our dispatcher a little susceptible to Automation and Anchoring Bias... was our dispatcher's Situational Awareness appropriately elevated? Was her Review/Monitor/Predict loop firing on all cylinders? I decided: 'I'll set it aside for now--relax; captain--it's probably nothing.' (spoiler alert: it turned out to be nothing but the ambiguity of whether it was or not; before and during the flight was distracting.)I do not have the ATIS but I did reconstruct the conditions from a historical AWOS archive01 XA:51Z: 330/19G26 6SM -RASN BR BKN009 OVC016 02/01 A29.60 PK WND 3303301 XB:51Z: 330/18G27 3SM -SN BR BKN008 OVC013 02/01 A29.61 PK WND 3203201 XC:06Z: 330/16G29 3SM -SN BR BKN010 OVC015 02/00 A29.61 PK WND 3202901 XC:14Z: 320/16G24 2-1/2SM -SN BR BKN008 OVC 013 02/00 A29.61 PK WND 3202901 XC:23Z: 320/14G28 1-3/4SM -SN BR OVC010 01/00 A29.61 PK WND 3202901 XC:30Z: 330/18G22 1-3/4SM -SN BR BKN008 OVC012 01/00 A 29.61 PK WND 32029The ATIS stated ILS RWY XXL and RCC (Runway Condition Code) 5/5/5 at XJ:00Z; XXR/YYL and XY/YX CLSD; and variousCLSD taxiways. The RCC time stamp of XJ:Z caught my attention as being roughly 16+ hours old.[Aircraft type] landing crosswind component 'guidelines' in the Flight Manual Limitations Chapter:RCC 5 and 6: 40 knotsRCC 4: 35 knotsRCC 3: 25 knots'NOTE: The crosswind guidelines presented...... The use of these guidelines should be based on the current weather conditions and thepilot's ability and experience level.'Even with the recent uptick in flying the overwhelming majority of us are feeling 'rusty'. Generally; with my XX+ years flying experience it seems to come back fairly quickly but I definitely don't feel the same high level of finesse as I did a year ago. I'm aware of it; very aware. I'm working diligently to trap the errors and as minor as they seem there are more of them.Actively seeking and countering threats; setting the right tone on the flight deck; and ensuring we are working well with dispatch these are some of the best tools we have. Overconfidence bias especially in challenging conditions while none of us are flying as much as we usually do is probably at the top of the list. Going the extra step of resolving seemingly small ambiguities seems especially appropriate right now. Plan continuation bias; expectancy bias; and optimism bias creep into the mix a little more insidiously than usual. The ever-present threat of complacency has absolutely not waned. I wonder is Dispatch in the same boat? I suspect so. I want to be cognizant and understanding of that likelihood--we are a team and we need to lift each other up.A little over an hour prior to touchdown we began planning our ILS runway XX approach and landing with the most current conditions at the time. We reviewed the NOTAMs; assiduously marking up the myriad of closed taxiways on our -9 pages. We pulled landing performance data and reviewed it intently. With the strong direct and gusty crosswinds we might have preferred a flaps 40 landing on Runway YX but it was closed.I felt the RCC 5/5/5 at XJ:00Z (16+ hours old) was a remnant and if we just encouraged dispatch to make a call to ZZZ1 Tower; we could get something much more useful; more akin to an hourly observation. Actively countering the ambiguity of a very stale RCC given the worsening weather conditions. Making allowance that Dispatch is planning and monitoring somewhere between several and a small slew of flights they aren't going to catch everything. The ACARS reply from 'X Dispatch' for an updated RCC:'ZZZ1 CITY OPS SAYS 5/5/5.THEY WON'T UPDATE THE NOTAM/FICON UNTIL THERE IS SOMETHING SIGNIFICANT TO UPDATE.'Ambiguity not resolved. Concern we were being led down a path... heightened.With time for more nuanced decision points running out; I replied to X Dispatch:'Name; JUST WANT US ALL TO HAVE THE SAME UNDERSTANDING ABOUT HOW CRITICAL I VIEW A CURRENT AND ACCURATE FICON* WITH THE GUSTY DIRECT CROSSWIND TEMP/DP 02/01... I WILL NOT ATTEMPT A LANDING WITHOUT IT. IS THE 5/5/5 FROM XJ:00Z OR HAVE THEY MEASURED IT MORE RECENTLY... I WILL NOT ALLOW A GREEN BAY OFF THE ENDCOMPLACENCY FROM ZZZ1 CITY OPS TO SUFFICE. PLS INFO FODM OF THIS'(*FICON; I should have used 'RCC' instead)X Dispatch reply:'OKAY.I GOT THE [Flight Operations Manager] IN ON A CALL TO ZZZ1 TOWER. FODM IS UP TO SPEED ON THE CONCERN. THEY REPORTED: RWY XXL AT XS:54Z. 5/5/5. 100 PCT WET.AGAIN; THEY DO NOT ANTICIPATE TO UPDATE UNLESS THERE IS AN ACCUMULATION ON THE RUNWAY OR ANOTHER CHANGE IN CONDITION.'Did I feel like we were pushing a rope? Yes. Yes; I did. I felt like we were being dismissed and my confidence in X Dispatch was significantly diminished. I felt like X Dispatch was not proactively leaning forward in the saddle and was content to accept whatever ZZZ1 City Ops said. Rather than feeling supported; rather than feeling like the increased risk was being adequately considered and mitigated; I felt a certain inappropriate nonchalance had insidiously permeated. I feel like Ihad to kick up a ruckus to finally get an RCC 5/5/5 at XC:00Z--as the XS:54Z RCC was still 7-ish hours old... DURING a RASN event. Finally; I feel like the fact that nothing bad happened adds to the confirmation bias that it was much ado about nothing... right up until it's not nothing. And 'Not Nothing' happens in a blazingly fast moment.What could I have done differently and more effectively?I should have called X Dispatch from ZZZ7 on leg #2; asking her to work up our leg #3 early--I could have alleviated the time compression I felt on the ground in ZZZ.In retrospect; I wish I had noted the XJ:00Z RCC from the ATIS available in either FD Pro X or just pulled the ZZZ1 ATIS on ACARS before pushing and added resolving the stale RCC to the fuel increase phone call with X Dispatch.I should have used 'X' words in the ACARS messages with dispatch to convey the level of my unease.I should have started descent planning and landing in those conditions at Top of Climb (TOC) to allow more time to get 'the ball rolling'.I should have taken the time to call after the flight; to debrief with our dispatcher... really wish I had done that.Ongoing concerns/questionsX Dispatch:1. How early can I ask for a flight to be planned?2. What fuel contingency plan is appropriate in these meteorological conditions for delayed arrival (E.G.; holding while treating the runway) or the preceding aircraft doesn't clear the runway in a timely manner or a gusty wind event causes an unstabilized approach condition? To name but a few possibilities...3. What could I have done differently from X Dispatch's perspective? I would like the feedback to make me a better Captain.ATC:1. How long before an RCC gets updated? Is there an upper number of hours? 2 hours? 36 hours?2. In these particular conditions of strong gusty crosswinds and rain snow mix; what is an appropriate update interval to give us 'end-users' a reasonable level of confidence in the data?3. How are RCCs actually determined?I am going to end this unusual report with a few words from a recently would-have-been furloughed-but-took-advantage-of-voluntary-separation-program and much-liked coworker. It's important to recognize that Name was an administrative co-worker. He's not a pilot himself but he certainly recognized how important his part in the team mattered. We could all profit from his example. 'I made it my goal to make sure every pilot left the flight planning area with a clear head. No matter what issue they had coming in the door; when they went out to fly that issue was taken care of or they knew that I would do my best to take care of it and follow up with them on itsoutcome. I did not want to be the one to cause a distraction. I was proud to be the one who cleaned up the distractions.''...cleaned up distractions' ... Indeed.

Have more of a warning from ATC of the turbulence expected to encounter. We did not know of the severity of the turbulence until we were in it ourselves. Experienced continuous moderate turbulence between 8;000 feet to 3;000 feet on descent on the RNAV arrival. We landed without incident.

There was some confusion going into work as I was notified at XA:00 pm for my XA:30 shift  and told not to report to work until XD:45 due to Level 3 cleaning that was taking place. When we arrived at work the control floor was empty and ZAU ARTCC resumed operations at XD:55am with no briefings at all. I was working the MID configuration which includes the following sectors (sector numbers removed) along with the following approach controls (ZZZ1/ZZZ2/ZZZ3/ZZZ4/ZZZ5). During my stint which started at XF:00 am and ending at XI:00 am around XX:50 ZULU I spoke with Aircraft X looking for the ILS to Runway X at ZZZ2. I asked the pilot if he had the current weather at the field along with the NOTAMs and the pilot responded with a yes to the weather and that he had read the NOTAMs yesterday. I then read what I thought was all the pertinent NOTAMs to the aircraft. I vectored Aircraft X for the ILS at ZZZ2 and cleared the aircraft in for the approach. I switched the aircraft to advisories and requested a timely cancellation due to a second aircraft being vectored for the same approach. Aircraft X soon cancelled his IFR and reported a down time along with the condition of the runway and that the ramp area was in a much worse condition. I inadvertently missed the snow removal NOTAM and was later on informed by management that Aircraft X had landed on a runway with snow removal equipment on it. Any usage of the active runway should require call to either the facility or on frequency to prevent any confusion on what is going on at the airport. Any and all NOTAMs that directly effect the runways should be better identified. ZZZ2 had over 15 NOTAMs listed at the airfield which all look identical; if the ones like snow removal were BOLD or even a different color it may help the next person avoid the same mistake I made.
Report_2_Narrative: I was notified today upon arrival to work that Aircraft X had landed on a runway occupied by snow removal equipment at the time. I was unaware that this had happened. I was working the assist position when the aircraft was cleared for the visual approach. The Radar Controller had issued the weather and read the NOTAMs to the aircraft and neither of us were aware that snow removal was taking place at the airport. The aircraft canceled IFR prior to landing. I was not made aware of the hazardous situation until the following work shift. This is the third report that I have written on this type of event at this airport! Several years ago; I myself had an aircraft execute a missed approach due to snow removal equipment on the same runway. I of course submitted a report detailing the situation and offered recommendations to prevent the situation from occurring again. I received no feedback. I told my Operations Manager; who was aware of the incident; and was dismissed. I then took it to the local safety council who actually looked into it. However; after some time I was informed that the current procedures would remain in effect. No definite reason was given. If a runway is possibly occupied by snow removal equipment; an aircraft should not be cleared for an approach. Having an aircraft switch frequencies to contact the snow removal crew themselves to land obviously has many drawbacks. The snow removal crew should simply notify the control facility prior to entering the runway. ATC could then close the runway until notified that the snow removal crew has exited the runway. This could be done by radio or phone; thus preventing the situations that have previously occurred.

Report_1_Narrative: We were at FL410 in continuous light chop. The seat belt sign was on before we entered the known area of turbulence and remained on till we passed the turbulence. ATC [was] reporting light chop for the next 150 miles. I had the speed intervene window open. The N1 was set for turbulence penetration. The speed was Mach .76. Within a matter of seconds we were in severe mountain wave. The speed increased to Mach .80 and we were in the over speed band. The over speed clacker went off right away. We began losing altitude. I immediately reduced thrust which was ineffective so pulled the speed brake. The speed slowed to Mach .76. We lost 300 ft. quickly. Then the second wave hit and we climbed rapidly back to altitude then 200 ft. above the assigned altitude. The aircraft returned to FL410. The event was probably 30 to 45 seconds. During the event I had the FO (First Officer) seat the flight attendants immediately. I had the FO ask for a descent to FL390. We were given the descent. The FO reported the mountain wave and the trends to ATC. There was no traffic in the area on TCAS and none was reported by ATC. We reached FL390 with no further wave but the light turbulence continued. I checked the flight attendants in and they reported no one was up during the event. I reported the event to Maintenance as severe mountain wave and over speed. I reported the event to Dispatch as well. In my 25 years of flying I have never seen mountain wave this bad.

Took off from ZZZ in VFR and picked up IFR plan and was given clearance to 190. After passing through a thin layer I encountered a very large build up and the base was lower than the MEA. I was concerned for the safety of flight and descended below the MEA in order to clear the buildup. I did this without calling center. Center was very upset and canceled my flight plan. After clearing the buildup I began my climb again and was reissued my clearance.

I was on an IFR flight plan to ZZZ descending out of 7000 to 4000 while talking to approach control. It was solid IMC. Tops were 7200; bases were 3800. While descending through 5500 I noted another aircraft on TCAS about 5 miles same altitude traveling toward me also descending. I queried ATC and they said he was a VFR aircraft not talking to ATC. I told ATC it appeared we were closing rapidly; I was given a 30 degree left turn which I immediately performed. The other aircraft at the same time seemed to turn toward me and kept descending. We were within a few miles; I then got a TCAS traffic alert. I started another left turn but the aircraft continued its track toward me. My TCAS showed our aircraft at same altitude and on top of each other on the screen. I then started a climb. I estimate the aircraft passed behind me and we missed by less than 50 feet. I then landed without any other issue. I told ATC that I feel that was real close and I was going to file a NASA report. When I cancelled my IFR on the ground control asked me to call them. I did and spoke to the controller. He verified that there was a loss of separation and that the aircraft was VFR and not talking to ATC. He was able to give me the aircraft type and tail number. I was able to find out who the pilot flying was and I called him to discuss. He said he was VFR; saw a mile wide hole in the clouds and wanted to descend which is when he turned toward me and descended. He said at one point he had me in sight but it was solid IMC. He does not have an instrument rating..
Encountered severe ice and could not exit the conditions before losing ability to maintain altitude. Continued on with the approach to landing starting the approach 400 feet low.

I was Check Airman conducting Captain IOE on flight DCA-ZZZ1 . Light Snow Pellets falling at the time with small accumulations observed on aircraft surfaces. Decision made to de-ice type 1 only since we were number one for takeoff and de-ice pad is near departure end of runway 1 at DCA. We received our type 1 application; the Post de-icing report was received; and we taxied out and departed within specified holdover time. Takeoff was uneventful with no abnormalities noted.After landing in ZZZ1 a deadheading [DCA Pilot] based Captain came into the cockpit and presented to me a picture of our left wing immediately following de-icing. This picture showed significant accumulations of ice on the rear of the wing. The discussion we had was that the de-ice crew in DCA did a very cursory job of de-icing the aircraft and merely pushed the sum total of accumulation that was on the wing to the rear of the wing without melting it or pushing it off the wing. This calls into question a number of issues to include the competence and training of the de-ice personnel as well as whether the fluid used was at the correct ratio and temperature. Unfortunately; I do not have a picture of the right wing for comparison purposes.Immediately following this flight and within minutes of being made aware of what occurred; the new Captain and I went to the ZZZ1 Chief Pilots office and met with Chief Pilots [names] so they could communicate to the DCA station to make certain no additional airplanes left that day without being properly de-iced and that I would follow up with a report. [Reporter stated the cause of the incident was] DCA de-ice personnel not properly trained and supervised.More training and oversight of station de-ice personnel.

After pushback from gate in BWI; the ground crew removed the steering bypass pin.  They did not verify the bypass handle had returned to the normal steering position.  Pushback was stopped early because tug began to slide on the ramp.  We disconnected within 10 feet of the normal engine start position.  After engine start as I attempted to taxi due to a very slippery ramp condition we immediately began to slide without nose wheel steering.  I stopped the aircraft and reported poor to nil braking on the ramp thinking the slide was caused by outside conditions.  We advised ATC we were holding our position.  Maintenance came out to the aircraft and after plugging in reported that the bypass lever had inadvertently been stuck in the bypass position after the pin was removed.  The aircraft had been on the ground for four to five hours in freezing precipitation.  We had deiced at the gate but I am unsure if remaining frozen contamination caused the lever to not spring back to the normal position.  We advised ATC that we had found a stuck bypass lever and were now ready to taxi.  Ramps and taxiways were poor as [Air Carrier] had an Aircraft X slide off Taxiway F across from the Midfield Freight area.

I departed ZZZ with the intent to ferry it to ZZZ1.  At the time of departure; weather was reported as overcast at 1;800 feet; visibility 10SM.  Field elevation is 2;500 feet.  Highest obstacle of the enroute corridor was indicated as 3;325 feet; per ForeFlight profile.  Reported weather allowed for a 1;000 foot clearance of the highest obstacle.  Upon departing and climbing to 3;200 feet; I could clearly see over all the hill tops along my intended flight path.  Approximately 10 minutes into the flight; clouds started to form and spill over from hill tops to the north and west into the adjoining valleys.  I executed a turn to avoid the clouds.  I decided I would return to ZZZ.  Suddenly; clouds started to form all around me.  The area around me was mountainous covered with thick forest. I felt that IMC was imminent so I initiated the IMC procedure.  I leveled the aircraft and started a climb; after adjusted power & airspeed settings.  I passed through the bottoms of 2 clouds as I started to set up the GPS for an instrument approach into ZZZ.  A moment later I was in a pocket clear of clouds with a large field next to a residence below me.  I decided it better to land VFR in the field than to continue IFR back to ZZZ.  I performed a descending orbit towards the field to get a clear picture of any/all possible obstacles.  A safe landing was performed without incident.  Notifications were made to Center.  After waiting approximately 2 hours; I was able to safely complete the ferry flight. Become IFR current within company.

It was the middle of a very heavy snow [storm] and strong winds. We made it to the de-icing pad and were de-iced and had anti-icing applied (100% Type IV). I noticed on the way to the runway that the side cockpit windows were still snow covered. In fact; they did not do any sort of de/anti-icing of the front of the aircraft. I told the Captain I wanted to call back to the de-icing crew and ask them about it; but the Captain said it didn't matter. So we took off after I did the wing inspection. As we accelerated in the climbout; we hear numerous large chunks of ice fly off the airplane; loud enough that I wrote the airplane up and told maintenance they needed to do an aircraft inspection after landing.  Upon further reflection; I am sure they did not de-ice the fuselage.  With heavy; wet snow falling; they should have.

I landed on Runway XX and my airspeed was approximately 76 knots. As soon as I touched ground; I slowed down in order to follow Controller directions. The Controller asked if I could make Taxiway Alpha X. By that point; I had already passed the taxiway and responded to the Controller in which I stated negative. The Controller's commands were to make a left turn at Taxiway Golf. I then proceeded to slow down and pulled back on the yoke to the turn left onto Taxiway Golf. Before fully exiting the runway; the tires of the plane became stuck on the snow ground and I was not able to move forward. The Controller questioned me and asked if I was able to move forward. I replied that I was unable to and that I was stuck in the snow. The Controller responded back and said hold tight; ground operations was on their way. As ground operations came; I shut off the plane and aided the ground operations staff in pushing the plane backwards. We successfully pushed the plane out of the snow.I started the plane back up and I was then able to make a U-turn back onto the Runway XX and taxi onto Alpha Y. The sign for Bravo (B) was not visible and I exited off of Alpha Y; made the left turn for Alpha and then right turn for Bravo.This experience taught me to avoid any taxiways that appear to have ice or snow on them. I also learned that I must make the judgement as being the pilot in command. As the pilot in command; I have the ability to deny requests made by Air Traffic Controller if I cannot adhere to their request promptly. I must note that it is important that all parties involved must remain safe; since safety is a priority of mine.

Flying ZZZ-ZZZ1 the flight experienced a severe updraft in clear air at FL270; which caused the airplane to climb 1;600 ft. despite any attempt to arrest the ascent for roughly 20 seconds. I instructed the Pilot Monitoring to request priority handling once it was evident that the altitude was uncontrollable. ATC responded that we were cleared down to FL250 when able. We regained control of our altitude and ensured that nobody was injured during the event. We determined that the event did not exceed any of the airplane limitations and continued on to ZZZ1.

Pre-flight planning with ForeFlight didn't show icing conditions where I encountered them in flight. The ZZZ1 live weather cams looked promising too. I should've used multiple resources instead of just ForeFlight. Years of multiple flights on this route gave me a false sense security. I was aware of a small storm moving in. I pushed my limits; trying to arrive before the storm while taking advantage of tail winds.These are all examples of what lead up to my problem. I encountered MVFR conditions over the mountains approximately 40 miles southwest of ZZZ2 at approximately 14;500 ft. I noticed light rime icing; air temp -20c. I climbed and notified ATC I would return to ZZZ due to weather. This was a safe decision. While Heading back at 17;500 ft.; it appeared I could make it safely over the top of the weather. I planned to overfly ZZZ2 and look for a safe opening in the weather. I had done this many times before and was still feeling complacent and confident. I informed ATC of my plans to turn around. This was NOT a safe or intelligent decision and caused my problem. I should have acknowledged my original weather concerns and continued back home. While close to ZZZ2 clouds built up vertically rapidly. I started icing up and informed ATC of my situation and needed to climb to FL 18;500 ft. I was at my service ceiling; with few options. Despite pitot heat and MVFR; my pitot tube froze up. I lost all primary instruments; switched to secondary steam gauges; also my O2 tank malfunctioned. This all happened in a matter of minutes. Lack of practice on partial instrument panel failures did not help my situation. I informed ATC I was unable to copy IFR clearances at this time.Maneuvering around building IMC icing conditions; on steam gauges; at my service ceiling; without O2; and now fighting headwinds was very challenging. Flying the plane had to be my sole focus and ATC understood. ATC made me a priority aircraft for me due to my unwillingness to copy my IFR clearance. As I headed southwest my instruments came back online and the weather improved. I subsequently picked up an IFR clearance. I requested lower; plus cancellation of IFR from ZZZ Center. ZZZ Center asked if I was still in distress. I assured ATC everything was fine now. I was no longer considered an aircraft in a priority handling situation. As a preventative safety measure; planes without deicing equipment should not fly near icing conditions even in VMC. Traditional VFR cloud separation rules; while legal are not always safe; especially when potentially hazardous conditions exist. A good preventative measure would have been not to fly at all that day.

Landed in ZZZ was normal. Visibility was 1/2 mile fog snow RVR 2;400 feet. Runway code was 3/3/3 and the previous landing reported fair braking. We landed flaps 30 auto brake 3. The airplane slowed down normal. Had to roll to the end due to taxiway closures. As we approached the end; I told my First Officer [we] needed to go slow in case it was slick. As we turned off [Runway] XXL the plane started to slide in the turn. Used left engine thrust reverse to help. Hit 3 taxi lights we did not leave the pavement. Taxied to the ramp notified ATC of the situation and was told we hit 3 taxi lights. Called and notified Dispatch and Maintenance Control. Made logbook entry. On walk around no aircraft damaged noted.
Report_2_Narrative: After an otherwise normal landing in ZZZ; the aircraft started to slide on a patch of ice as the Captain was turning off the runway and onto the taxiway. Although the aircraft never departed the paved surface; we were uncertain if the slide caused any damage to the taxiway lights. We informed ground control of the slide and the possibility of striking a taxiway light. Ground control notified the airport operations to have them take a look. They reported back that there were indeed 3 taxi lights down. Weather conditions at the time were 1/2 mile visibility with snow and fog. The [Runway] XXL runway condition code was reported as 3/3/3. We landed with auto brake 3 and the aircraft slowed normally. ATC requested a turnoff at the end of the runway due to numerous taxiway closures. It was at the end of the runway while turning off that the aircraft entered into the slide. No damage to the aircraft was found by either the flight crew or Maintenance upon inspection of the aircraft at the ramp.Unfortunately despite the rest of the runway having adequate braking; the runway end was incredibly slick. The only suggestion that could've prevented this would have been to make the turn at an even slower speed in order to possibly prevent the slide.

Report_1_Narrative: After having sent 2 test balloons (party balloons with helium) to check winds and finding they both showed no more than 7 knots of wind and practically went straight up as far as I could see them. Only clouds in the visible area were small white cumulus approximately 10;000 feet high. I chose to launch my hot air balloon near in a dirt lot with no obstruction down wind and more than enough room to move around in case of wind gusts. Knowing I might experience some gusts of wind due to only one local airport having seen gusts up to 14 knots. During my entire set up and inflation; I hadn't experienced any gusts more than 10 knot. After inflating the balloon to an upright position and the balloon maintaining its usual shape and taking pressure as normal I asked the passengers to load into the balloon basket. I was still tied to the van with a 5/8' polyester yacht braided rope and started heating the balloon with a fuel tank only used for the purpose of inflation to buoyancy.Before I could heat the balloon to buoyancy the wind hit the balloon from the opposite direction pushing me toward the passenger van westbound. Then hit me again heading east at an estimated 30 knots while dragging the balloon to the end of the tie down rope. The rope then broke and the balloon began to drag across the large lot. I gave full power to the burners trying to heat the balloon into flight knowing I wouldn't be able to stop the balloon on the ground in that speed of wind before hitting something. We started to lift off the ground approximately 10 to 15 feet off the ground and the balloon started descending again toward a chain link fence. Once hitting the fence was inevitable I started opening the deflation port. It took an additional acre of land for the balloon to come to a complete stop on another chain link fence.After this was all over the wind had completely stopped; and had relatively calm winds the rest of the evening. All of the small cumulus clouds were completely gone after this incident with clear skies. Making me assume those clouds had dissipated causing outflow gusts of at least 30 knots. In total hitting 3 chain-link fences. All passengers and myself were unharmed with no injuries and no damage to the balloon to cause it to be unairworthy.
