I was at FL240 enroute from ZZZ to ZZZ1 when the aircraft started picking up ice. There had been PIREP reports of ice between 14;000 to 18;000 and 22;000 to 24;000.  I requested to descend and was given 22;000 by ATC. I was still picking up significant ice at 22;000 and requested lower altitude. The ice was continuous and becoming moderate at 20;000 and the aircraft was accumulating a significant amount of ice. I had to keep descending and while trying to control the aircraft; I got into a left turn bank thereby deviating from the assigned heading. The ice piled on extremely quickly and it wasn't until I got around 15;000 that I finally got out of the ice and got back on course heading westbound. Although I was in significant ice; I believe my attention became too focused on the ice accumulation thereby distracting me from maintaining the heading and also assigned altitude.

The weather enroute to ZZZ1 wasn't too bad. I would characterize the clouds as scattered around 9;000 to 1;0000 feet with periodic cells of precipitation. Low temperatures meant icing conditions so we avoided all precipitation. Preflight planning weather briefing was in agreement with what we were observing; and we listened to each Automated Weather of the airports we passed.The area was nearly clear skies. Few clouds if any and no observed precipitation within 20 miles. Winds and clouds were moving east; following a cold front which passed the day prior. We encountered one broken cell at 10;000 feet off our left and few clouds off our right. We climbed to 11500 feet and passed above and well clear of that cell within a short period of time.We stayed VFR; away from known icing conditions and moisture. The ride was uneventful. On the ride back; I expected the same weather. Conditions at my destination were VFR with scattered clouds up high; and a nearby airport was reporting the same. I did not check one of the airports enroute.  Radar returns on the map showed the same few cells scattered without only light precipitation. In the same region we encountered what I believed to the be the same cell we crossed over in the previous flight. We took note of precipitation left; right and ahead to the left. With a large enough gap of scattered clouds ahead to the right. We continued; and I made the decision to climb up to 10;500 feet to determine the height of the clouds. The clouds which were still only 500 feet in height; were now based around 10;500 feet.I made the decision then to climb to 12;500 feet to cross over them. I knew we were less than 30 minutes from an area which previously was nearly clear skies. Upon reaching 12;500 feet; I started the timer and we continued to cross well above the layer. I would describe this layer to be 6/8 to 7/8 as there were often breaks where we could see ground and see that the layer was not very thick in height. Roughly 10 to 15 miles; 5 to 8 minutes we noticed the clouds were becoming higher altitude; and we were losing spacing from them. We maneuvered around them as best we could; but would need to climb to stay above them and away from icing conditions. I absolutely knew that we would not be putting the airplane into known icing conditions. At this point; turning back didn't seem to be any more promising than continuing on course. We had confidence that the weather in the area was better beyond this cell; and we just witnessed the weather make a change of heart behind us.At this point; I knew that things could turn worse. As contingency; we began direct course for an airport with good weather. In the event that we reached 30 minutes above 12;500 feet or encountered any ice; we would immediately descend through the nearest break and land the airplane.As we continued; the clouds continued to rise. Still broken and overcast beneath us; we were faced with few options. Climb higher; enter known icing; or drop through a break and hope there is not precipitation around which would become ice.Entering icing conditions was not an option; or if anything a very last resort. Hypoxia was our biggest threat at altitude; and we were well aware and cautious of any symptoms. Luckily; I have had the experience of an altitude chamber and know my symptoms and signs. But we had to climb higher to avoid the icing conditions. We climbed up to 14;500 feet briefly and descended back down when able. Then the weather cleared up; the clouds became scattered; and we were able to descend back down to 8;500 feet. A total time of 24 minutes was observed above 12;500 feet. I would estimate that only 2-3 minutes were spent above 14;000 feet. No signs or symptoms of hypoxia were observed. The remainder of the flight was VFR and uneventful.The challenge was the lack of weather reporting equipment to determine the weather enroute; but this is not an excuse. I will exercise more resources such as a phone briefing or additional charts next time. I will also remember to never expect the weather to remain the same. Next time I will remember my favorite motto; Plan for the worst; and hope for the best.

Aircraft X was an arrival into PVU airport. He came to my sector from the northwest.  I had to vector the aircraft to get him in trail of SLC arrivals. I turned the aircraft in trail of a SLC B752.  As I turned the aircraft further north so final turn wasn't so tight; the pilot called the airport in sight. PVU was advertising Visual Approach Runway 13. I cleared the aircraft for the Visual Approach. They turned towards the airport; I shipped them to the Tower approximately 10 miles out when I saw them turning towards the airport. Almost immediately the aircraft started turning further south towards a 8;900 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude descending out of 8000 feet. I called PVU and they said they were talking to him. I asked what they were doing and was advised the pilot stated they were making S turns or something. The aircraft descended to 5;100 feet 7 miles from the airport; elevation 4;497 feet.  At that point PVU called stating the pilot had lost the airport and was climbing to 9;000 feet. The pilot checked in with me heading 180 and climbing to 9;000 feet. I said roger. They then asked for vectors to an ILS and I stated I would when they were at Minimum Vectoring Altitudes. I vectored for the ILS with no further complications. I don't really have recommendations on my end. PVU after this incident continued to advertise Visual Approaches. My Supervisor did not want to fill out any paperwork on the incident; so they said nobody did anything wrong and to leave it at that. I felt like this should be reported somehow. The pilot was extremely low a long distance from the airport trying to scud run the Visual Approach. I knew visuals were iffy because when I descended the aircraft to 9;000 feet they were IMC; I turned them and descended them to 8;000 feet (also telling them to expect the ILS) and they called the airport. It's not surprising they were able to call the airport in sight at that point; the mountain sits below and often they can descend slightly and get the airport in sight. What's surprising is the pilot accepted the approach if clouds were that bad; that we didn't 'declare an emergency' for the aircraft when they lost the airport at 5;100 feet in an 8;900 foot Minimum Vectoring Altitude MVA; and that the supervisor wouldn't at least want to follow up with the pilot. I truly don't know why we wouldn't want to look at this further.

After landing at ZZZ; during taxi from Runway XXL; the intersection at [Taxiway] 1 and 1X was braking action extremely poor. The Captain was unable to slow to turn and drifted through the intersection. With snow plows in the area to clear a route back to Taxiway 1 via 1Y; it was safer to not turn on the snow and ice and request the plows to clear a path to the Ramp.Cause - Poor taxiway conditions. Suggest quicker plowing at the Airport taxiways.
Report_2_Narrative: Landed ZZZ Runway XXL. Weather was near mins. ATIS was reporting RCC 5/5/5; with taxiways and aprons covered with snow covered icy patches. Prior aircraft reported braking action med-poor. The FO and I ran the numbers; after considering elevation; gross weight; and reported braking; the APS showed plenty of room to brake with a 1X;000 feet runway. After landing; we also reported 'medium to poor' braking action to Tower with the midfield having the poorest braking action. We taxied clear and had to advise Tower of our exit and position as visibility continued to drop. Clearance was given to taxi to ramp via [Taxiway] 2; left onto [Taxiway] 3; cross [Runway] XXR; right onto [Taxiway] 1 to 1Z; report clear of XXR. Taxi was slow as the taxiways had icy patches. We were also advised that the prior aircraft had taken 30 mins to taxi to their gate. On making left turn from [Taxiway] 2 to 3; it was readily apparent that the taxiways were indeed slippery as even a turn at 5 knots still allowed some traction issues. Upon nearing XXR westbound; we noticed XXR and [Taxiway 1] appeared snowed over (not cleared). After advising tower that we were clear XXR; and upon applying brakes for the turn northbound onto [Taxiway 1]; it became obvious the braking action was worse on the west side of the airport.  Once the aircraft came to a complete stop; we were past the centerline of [Taxiway] 1; or at least the best we could tell as the taxiways were covered with fresh snow (blue taxiway lights were visible). On a dry day with fillets; it would still be doable to make the turn. But with the taxiways in worse condition than I've ever experienced; we relayed to Tower the taxiway conditions and whether we could instead proceed straight via [Taxiway] X1 to X3 as the snow plow trucks were clearly visible on [Taxiway] X2. Tower advised the ground crew to clear [Taxiway] X2 to X3 and cleared us to the ramp.Cause - Bad weather; mainly icing under snow covered runways and taxiways.Allow PIREPs to more directly affect the RCC reported on ATIS. Even after we reported medium to poor braking action after the previous landing aircraft had reported the same; the ATIS was still indicating RCC of 5/5/5. This in my opinion is misleading and contrary to the whole purpose of reporting RCC's.

Report_1_Narrative: We were cleared for taxi to Runway 1. We exited the ramp at Taxiway D and proceeded southbound on Taxiway A. In order to maximize available runway; we followed the second (southernmost) curved taxiway center line onto Taxiway A1; toward the active runway. We observed the enhanced taxiway center line markings; however as the Taxiway A transitioned to Taxiway A1 the hold-short line was completely obscured by packed snow.We stopped the aircraft approximately 60 feet past what we estimate to be the location of the hold-short line and immediately notified the Tower of our error. We were instructed to perform a 180 back to Taxiway A. The incursion did not present any immediate conflict to other aircraft. Another aircraft was simultaneously taxiing on the active runway for the north de-icing pad. The nearest arriving aircraft was greater than 10 miles away and had not yet been cleared to land.Upon further analysis of the error; we (the crew) determined that the short taxi certainly contributed to the confusion. The Holding Position sign for RWY 1-19 is situated on the northern edge of Taxiway A1 and angled to the northeast; in a manner that makes it very easy to miss when entering Taxiway A from Taxiway D. By the time the aircraft is established on Taxiway A; that sign is already abeam the aircraft. From the perspective of the pilot flying from the left seat; that sign is not visible in this situation. But the most significant factor in our misstep today was the nearly complete obscuration of the hold short line at the intersection of taxiways A and A1.

Aircraft X departed JNU for ZZZ; but changed destination shortly after to return to JNU because the pilot reported that ZZZ winds would not allow for an approach. Aircraft X initially climbed to 090; but requested descent to 080 for moderate icing. I cleared Aircraft X back to the JNU airport via direct SSR direct; to expect the LDA Z 8 approach; as requested.I cleared Aircraft X for the LDA Z 8 approach; to cross SSR at or above 060. Shortly after progressing SSR; the aircraft transmitted that they were back in the icing and had 'quite a bit of ice on the wing' and said they were unsure if they would be able to maintain the transition altitude of 056 until LYNNS intersection. The aircraft then descended to 052; below the approach altitude. I issued a low altitude alert; and instructed Aircraft X to climb to meet the transition altitude. Aircraft X reported they would attempt to climb; but were unable and appeared to level off at 050 in a 060 MIA (Minimum IFR Altitude); and requested to transition over to the RNAV W 8 approach via JIGMI; as the approach had a lower transition altitude. Seeing as the aircraft had already progress JIGMI; I asked if they would be able to establish themselves on to a published portion of the approach if I provided a heading to join. the pilot said affirmative; so I issued a 10 degree turn to the right to join the RNAV W 8 approach; and to steer the aircraft away form higher terrain to the NW; as it was clear they would be unable to climb. I then informed the pilot that I was providing priority handling for them. Aircraft X was able to maintain 050 until joining the RNAV W 8 approach; at which time I issued the approach clearance and confirmed that the aircraft would be able to maintain the new transition altitudes of that approach. The pilot confirmed that they would; and was able to safely fly the approach to landing.

We were in cruise flight in smooth air with the autopilot on and flying our cost index profile. The Captain was on his rest break. I was the non flying pilot in the left seat; and the Flying First Officer was in the right seat. We were at 37;000 ft. and M.83 in smooth air when the airspeed started climbing as we encountered a mountain wave. The flying First Officer immediately began to reduce the throttles and came all the way to idle as the airspeed kept increasing. At this point; I asked if he would like me to deploy the speedbrakes to assist in the effort to keep the aircraft from over speeding. I began deploying the speedbrakes; but the aircraft accelerated slightly above maximum operating to M.875. We maintained idle thrust and speed brakes until clear of the mountain wave.We then submitted a PIREP electronically and also notified ATC who said we were the first report. However; shortly thereafter another aircraft also reported mountain wave activity. Total airspeed fluctuation was plus or minus about 15 to 20 kts.

After landing I maintained center line; braking action after landing I noticed was nil. The plane was near stopping and had started to veer to the right; at the time in which I input left rudder to counter the right turn and no avail the aircraft began to slide sideways. After attempts were made to completely stop; the engines were shut down to prevent further damage as it slid into the snowbank to which after coming to a complete stop; ATC was notified once we were allowed to exit the aircraft noticed the runway was icy.
Report_2_Narrative: Upon touchdown on runway 25 at Merrill airport it was very apparent that there was NIL braking action available. Tower informed us prior to landing that there was no braking action reports. As we were decelerating through approximately 35-40 knots ground speed; the student in the left seat (pilot flying) commented that we had no brakes. I immediately placed my feet on the rudder pedals with him and applied brakes. There was no braking available. I released pressure from the brakes to aid in steering and asked the student to cease braking. My intentions at this time was to allow the aircraft to decelerate using aerodynamic braking and roll to the end of the runway. Shortly thereafter the aircraft began a slide to the right. I immediately applied full left rudder without any action. I then applied left brake to aid in the directional control; which also did not assist. My last resort was to increase power on the right engine to attempt to correct the aircraft towards center line; but at this point the aircraft was too far gone in the slide. As it was apparent the aircraft was going to exit the runway I immediately pulled both mixtures to the idle cutoff position in an effort to stop the props from rotating and causing any damage. The aircraft ended up approximately 110 degrees rotated to the right of the center of the runway and the aircraft came to a rest approximately 5-10 feet off of the runway edge in approximately 6 inch deep snow. At this point I informed the tower of our position and we would need assistance to get the aircraft back on the runway. I called the owner of [the FBO and he was able to remove and tow the aircraft without further incident. After further inspection it was determined that there was absolutely no damage to the aircraft or any airport property; including the runway edge lights. The aircraft has since been turned over to Angel Aviation maintenance for further inspections.

Report_1_Narrative: We landed in ZZZ with a Code 2 braking report. DOT had done a superb job of plowing and sanding the runway resulting in an actual braking action of at least Medium. It continued to snow during our approximate 30 minute turn while DOT continued to plow the runway. DOT stopped plowing when we commenced deicing. After our deicing and engine starts we commenced taxiing. Upon turning onto the runway the jet slid sideways across the runway. After correcting the slide I made a second turn onto the center line; experiencing a second slide. I determined Braking Action to be Nil. I set the parking brake and called DOT informing them we needed a complete plow and sanding of the runway. This took approximately 20 minutes. After DOT completed their work and issued a Braking Action report; we departed safely without incident.Crews should remain vigilant and remember Braking action changes rapidly with heavy snow.

During cruise flight attendants were seated by Captain due to continuous light chop with reports of moderate chop ahead. After close to an hour of flight attendants being seated; we experienced a momentary encounter with moderate mountain wave turbulence where the aircraft banked about 20 degrees and airspeed increased 10 to 15 kts. Event lasted about 10 seconds. Pilot spoke with FAs (Flight Attendants) and they all said they were fine as well as all passengers. Everyone was seated during the turbulence encounter. Later in the flight a FA said her neck had some pain and she thought it was due to the turbulence encounter earlier. She said she was ok to perform her duties for the remainder of the flight and for her return flight. Dispatch and the station were notified; and a FA supervisor met the aircraft to discuss the incident/injury with the flight attendant.

ATC advised of SIGMET R with reports of moderate turbulence. As we approached the forecast area of turbulence we slowed to turbulence penetration speed of .78 MACH at FL360. After a few minutes of mostly moderate turbulence with significant mountain wave turbulence... we abruptly encountered approximately 10 seconds of severe turbulence resulting in simultaneous 5 to 10 degrees roll variations; +/-10 knots airspeed variations; 5 degrees pitch variations and over 1000 fpm down resulting in approximately 100 ft. below altitude. Autopilot recover aircraft to level flight. No injuries or damage noted. Reported to ATC. A few minutes following our PIREP; another aircraft report severe turbulence. Clear Air Turbulence associated with mountain wave.Suggest better forecasting. Uber conservative routing around forecast turbulence.

Enroute from ZZZ to ZZZ1 at approximately ZZZZZ; we encountered chop at FL350 and decided to request FL390; which was granted. During the climb at approximately FL370 we encountered moderate turbulence. The airspeed increased into overspeed and the vertical speed increased significantly due to an updraft. Though I did not note the actual VS; I estimate at least 5;000 FPM based on the rate that the altitude tape was moving; which was where my focus was. I disconnected the autopilot; and as we went through FL380 I knew leveling at FL390 was going to be difficult because I couldn't safely decrease thrust any further; and pitching over would have made the overspeed worse. Using a little of each method; I was able to achieve a normal thrust setting and speed below max at approximately FL395 and returned to FL390; where we reported the event to ATC. We sent an overspeed code to Maintenance via ACARS. The sky conditions at the time were [VFR].
Report_2_Narrative: During climbout through FL370 we encountered an increase in both speed and climb while the B autopilot was engaged. After the PF (Pilot Flying) disengaged the autopilot the aircraft continued to increase in speed causing an over speed condition by approximately 10-15 kts. The aircraft continued to climb through our selected altitude; FL390; by approximately 400 ft. while the pilot flying was executing his recovery techniques to return the aircraft by to the correct altitude and speed. Approximately FL350 I had already asked the flight attendants to be seated because we had a small amount of turbulence. During the event; both the passengers and flight attendants were seated. I relayed the turbulence/updraft to air traffic control as severe in nature so that other aircraft wouldn't be routed through the area. After the event both the PF and myself felt that is was probably more of a moderate turbulence and more of a severe updraft. Shortly after; I checked on both the flight attendants and passengers to ensure no one was injured. The event was not jerky or real rough; it was more of a pretty smooth continual pitch up that was very difficult to stop without really over speeding the aircraft more than we did. I felt like the PF did as good of a good as possible in this strange situation.

Report_1_Narrative: Was doing a training flight for a rusty pilot returning to flight. Wind was about 270/17 [kts.] G24. We were taxiing from parking to Runway XX. Pilot seemed to be fine on the taxi. I was looking down trying to fix some static on my headset by readjusting the plugs. The aircraft rotated suddenly to the right and off the taxiway into the grass. I took control and taxied back onto the taxiway. No further issues. We ended up not flying due to the strong crosswind.My analysis was that the pilot was overpowered by a strong gust from the right that was strong enough to rotate the plane about 30 degrees to the right. The pilot hit full left rudder but did not apply brakes or try and counter with brakes due to his lack of experience. My fault as I should not have been heads down. He just got hit by a very strong gust and didn't react properly. The taxi back onto the taxiway was uneventful but I was a bit more observant!

We were taxiing from the landing runway to the gate and turning right onto Taxiway 1 from 2 when the airplane started sliding in the wrong direction (to the left); the Captain had the tiller going right however; the airplane still was going left so he applied the brakes and momentarily activated the right thrust reverser in an attempt to quickly stop the forward momentum of the airplane before sliding off the taxiway. He was able to get the airplane completely stopped while still on the taxiway with no further issues.We believe the taxiway was more slick than expected due to the wet conditions of the field.In the future; we think even slower taxi speeds would help; even though we were taxing at a good safe and slow speed at the time of the occurrence. Also; we would probably elect not to shutdown the number 1 engine in the future to help eliminate any chance of asymmetric thrust on wet taxiways.

On taxi out to Runway 34 at BGM airport there was blowing snow bringing the visibility down to almost 0. The only taxiway sign for taxiway L (Lima) was obscured. The yellow directional markings on ground were covered with snow. We missed the taxiway and slid off ramp just west of taxiway Lima(L). No injuries but substantial damage to nose gear.
Report_2_Narrative: Began taxi from ramp between Taxiways M and L.  While taxiing parallel to Bravo PF (pilot flying) called for taxi checklist.  PM (pilot monitoring) head was down for a brief period while doing taxi checks.  When PM looked up he told PF that something looked wrong about the aircraft position.  Almost immediately the aircraft entered the grass on the east side of Lima.  PF attempted to continue through grass to Bravo and the Aircraft dropped into a snow obscured swale and came to a stop.  Aircraft secured and evacuated.  2 crew and 2 passengers on board.  No injuries.

Report_1_Narrative: Instructor and Student were landing on [Runway] XXL at ZZZ airport. The braking conditions were 3/3/3. ATC instructed the student to taxi off of the runway at [Intersection] X1. With the snow it was a harder left turn on the ground; almost a back taxi for theX1 intersection from the runway. The aircraft was turned onto A8 but had to come to a full stop and this allowed for a loss in steering authority on the ground. When going to straighten the nose out to go from back taxiing to X1 to exiting X1; the aircraft brakes became unusable and the aircraft slid to the side of X1 where there was a pile of compacted snow which [prevented] a quick recovery [of] control. An aircraft landed on the runway while [we were] almost exited off the runway at X1. Tower came on and asked if we needed to be towed off and informed everyone that there was a disabled aircraft on the runway. There was no damage to airport property or the aircraft itself. No needed assistance.

A snow team had cleared snow from an intersecting runway and taxiway and had left snow on the runway. I asked the city vehicle about the snow on the runway and if it was safe for aircraft to depart. The city vehicle said the snow was 'just a dusting'. Two aircraft departed without incident. The snow team did another pass on the intersecting runway. After that; another aircraft started departure roll and then aborted the takeoff after hitting a snow bank. The pilot said it was about 2 feet deep and felt like hitting a herd of deer. The airport needs a vast improvement in their snow removal operation overall; and they need to be aware of snow that gets left on active runways.

I was the PF (Pilot Flying) on Aircraft X from ZZZ2-ZZZ1. I flew the RNAV RNP [Runway] XX approach with rain and wind in the vicinity. I attempted to configure early but a strong tailwind made it challenging. I encountered a wind gust which caused me to counter increasing airspeed by pitching up and slightly reduce power to maintain airspeed and continue configuring. This resulted in a position higher than anticipated. The PM (Pilot Monitoring) noticed this also and we articulated that. About that time; ATC directed a go-around. We executed the missed approach and climbed to 3;000 [feet] on vectors for another approach.Noting the conditions; I configured early for the second RNAV RNP XX approach. Rain and wind were still predominant throughout the approach but fewer gust excursions and we remained stable throughout the approach all the way to minimums. At minimums; we both had the approach lights and runway environment in sight and I continued the approach. As I turned final aligning with Runway XX; I lost sight of the runway environment due to the weather. The PM called go around and I initiated go around procedures. We climbed to 3;000 feet on vectors as directed by ATC. We communicated with one another and ensured we were both in the green; we had ample fuel; and agreed to attempt a third approach. On the third RNAV RNP XX approach; I configured early again and remained stable throughout the approach. The wind was still a factor though and required careful monitoring and aircraft correction to maintain a stable approach. Fully configured and in the vicinity of arriving at minimums; ATC advised a wind shift on the field of 330 degrees at 26 knots of wind. ATC asked for our intentions. Due to airline limitations on Maximum Tailwind Component for Landing; the PM told ATC we could not land and we would go-around. ZZZ1 Tower handed us off to ZZZ Departure and they advised us that they 'turned the airfield around' and they would give us vectors for the ILS RWY XY approach and we would be 'number 1' to the field. Again; the Captain and I communicated that we were both in the green; we noted that we had ample fuel; and we prepared for an ILS approach to Runway XY.After intercepting the localizer for the ILS to Runway XY; I configured fully and continued down the glideslope from the final approach fix. It was raining outside and was windy but we remained stable. Shortly prior to 1;000 feet AGL; it grew increasingly difficult to maintain a stable approach. I told the PM that I didn't feel comfortable; the weather was worsening; and it was likely that we would not be stable at 1;000 feet. We agreed and elected to go-around. Just as I began the go around procedure; we encountered a Wind Shear Warning and I proceeded with the Wind Shear escape maneuver. We exited the conditions quickly and transitioned to ATC's directives. We quickly agreed to divert to ZZZ3 and the PM informed ATC of our intention to divert. We climbed to 3;000 feet and flew vectors to ZZZ3.We flew the ILS RWY XYC approach and landed safely at ZZZ3 and taxied to the gate. We shut down the aircraft with approximately 7.4k pounds of fuel remaining. During the deplaning process; the Captain coordinated with the Maintenance and Dispatch and agreed to continue on to ZZZ1 as the weather cleared the area. We added fuel departing with 11.8k pounds of fuel. The Captain flew as the PF and I was the PM for the return flight to ZZZ1. The Captain maintained appropriate communication throughout the event. He communicated with the passengers explaining as best as possible what occurred and what we were doing to ensure their safety while doing our best to land at our intended destination. He kept the flight attendants apprised of the situation after each approach and we discussed each approach; verified we were both in the green; and maintained situational awareness at all times.
Report_2_Narrative: Flew RNAV RNP [Runway] XX approach. FO (First Officer) was PF (Pilot Flying) and was configuring the aircraft early. He was at gear down; flaps 15; and we encountered a wind gust that drove the airspeed indication almost to redline. The FO raised nose slightly and reduced power to slow the airspeed in order to continue configuring the aircraft. As this occurred; aircraft got a little high. As PM; I noticed this and was in process of informing FO when ATC said that we looked a little high and to just go around. We executed the missed approach as directed by ATC and climbed on a heading to 3;000 ft. to vector for another approach.On the second RNAV RNP XX; FO configured the aircraft early and shot approach to minimums. At minimums; approach lights and runway lights were visible; but as we turned to final approach; visibility was lost.  I called go-around; and FO initiated go-around. We climbed on a heading to 3;000 feet. We communicated with each other that we were both in the green; had plenty of fuel; and prepared for another approach.On the third RNAV RNP XX; the FO had fully configured the aircraft; when ATC advised that the wind had shifted out of the north at 330 degrees and we had 26 knots of wind at our tail. ATC asked our intentions; I told them that we could not land with those winds and that we were going around. At that point; ATC handed us off to departure; and departure immediately advised us that they had turned the airport around and could give us an immediate left downwind to be number 1; shooting ILS Runway XY. FO and I communicated with each other that we were both in the green; had plenty of fuel; and prepared for an approach using the new runway.On attempt to Runway XY; we intercepted localizer and G/S. We had continual high winds indicating to me that the aircraft would not be able to remain stable throughout approach and landing. As PIC; I called for a go-around. FO communicated to me his agreement; and I immediately notified Tower of our intention to divert to ZZZ3. FO initiated the miss. As he did; we encountered a brief Wind Shear Warning. FO finished cleaning aircraft up and we diverted to ZZZ3 without incident.We were vectored to ZZZ3 and landed on Runway XYC and taxied to the gate with 7.4 pounds of fuel. I called Dispatch and she said we had plenty of fuel to get to ZZZ1 but as a precaution due to the frontal weather passage; she added more fuel and brought us up to 11.8 pounds. After the front passage; I flew the aircraft as PF uneventfully to ZZZ1. During all of the approaches and the divert I kept the flight attendants up to date and communicated with the passengers at all times. We exercised good situational awareness and followed SOPs during the entire flight.

Report_1_Narrative: Was in Ground effect and slightly wheel touch; all of a sudden aircraft veer right; probably due to a strong gust of wind. I immediately jump into action to prevent any tipping from over correction. Unfortunately I could not save it in time; so it went off into the dirt and knick one blade and hit a runway marker.

Prior to entering Class D airspace at ZZZ; I found the AWOS system was down. Upon contact with ATC; they gave an altimeter only and asked my preferred runway. I chose [Runway] 'XX' based on my latest wind report. I landed on 'XX' and during roll-out I was hit by severe windshear. It lifted my right wing; I still had full flaps; and became airborne again. I was just off the landing strip pavement. I corrected the flight path; flew through a small bush; and finished the landing in the dirt alongside the runway. Tower advised me to taxi to transient parking and report to Tower. Upon exiting the airplane; I noticed damage to the prop. During the interview with the airport supervisor; we noticed the windsock changing directions frequently. It was a hot day in the mountains at 6000 ft. MSL; more turbulence was expected.
